metadata.file_name: 105. Stiff et al. (2023). Research engagement in the Progress in International Reading Literacy Study: A systematic review.pdf
metadata.title: Research engagement in the Progress in International Reading Literacy Study: A systematic review

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: This paper is a systematic review of PIRLS-related research articles rather than an analysis of PIRLS microdata. The authors describe a literature search across ERIC, PsycINFO, Scopus, Web of Science, and Zetoc, followed by screening and coding of included peer-reviewed journal articles. Because no student-level dataset is analyzed, there is no application of PIRLS sampling weights, replicate weights, or any complex survey design adjustments. Consequently, weight variables (e.g., W_FSTUWT/TOTWGT) are not applicable to the review’s methodology.

data.handling_not_reported_explanation: Plausible values (PVs) are not applicable because this study does not perform predictive modeling on PIRLS achievement outcomes; it synthesizes and categorizes findings from previously published PIRLS-related articles. The review’s unit of analysis is the included publication (coded by review foci), not student responses with PV-based reading achievement scores. Missing-data handling therefore refers to the review screening/coding process rather than imputation/deletion of missing values in an ILSA dataset, and no PV/missing-data strategy is described for microdata.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Not applicable to student-level sampling. The review identifies PIRLS-related peer-reviewed journal articles published between 2004 and 2022 by searching multiple bibliographic databases (ERIC, PsycINFO, Scopus, Web of Science, Zetoc) using PIRLS-related terms, restricting the date range for the update (Jan 2015–May 2022), removing duplicates, and then applying exclusion criteria: only English-language peer-reviewed journal articles were retained; conference proceedings were excluded; articles were excluded if PIRLS was not a main data source/topic based on abstract and full-text term checks; and articles using PIRLS materials on smaller independent samples or providing commentaries were excluded.

main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PIRLS the study leveraged the reported predictors to predict Reading Literacy, finding that This paper is a systematic review that synthesizes how PIRLS has been used in peer-reviewed research, rather than training or evaluating machine learning models on PIRLS student data. The authors report descriptive trends in the number and types of PIRLS-related articles over time (e.g., increased research engagement, especially after the 2011 cycle) and summarize qualitative themes in three areas: teacher characteristics and instruction practices, socioeconomic predictors of reading outcomes, and critiques of PIRLS scaling/IRT practices. They find that the proportion of secondary data analysis articles has increased since 2015, with a reduction in critique articles, while major topic areas remain relatively stable. No predictive performance metrics (e.g., accuracy/AUC/RMSE) are reported because the study does not conduct an empirical ML analysis. This indicates that ith a reduction in critique articles, while major topic areas remain relatively stable. No predictive performance metrics (e.g., accuracy/AUC/RMSE) are reported because the study does not conduct an empirical ML analysis.

data.outcome_summary: This paper is a systematic review that synthesizes how PIRLS has been used in peer-reviewed research, rather than training or evaluating machine learning models on PIRLS student data. The authors report descriptive trends in the number and types of PIRLS-related articles over time (e.g., increased research engagement, especially after the 2011 cycle) and summarize qualitative themes in three areas: teacher characteristics and instruction practices, socioeconomic predictors of reading outcomes, and critiques of PIRLS scaling/IRT practices. They find that the proportion of secondary data analysis articles has increased since 2015, with a reduction in critique articles, while major topic areas remain relatively stable. No predictive performance metrics (e.g., accuracy/AUC/RMSE) are reported because the study does not conduct an empirical ML analysis.
data.null_fields_interpretation: This is a systematic review of PIRLS-related publications and does not analyze PIRLS microdata with predictive modeling. Therefore, there is no student analytic sample size, no PV-based achievement modeling, no ML algorithm evaluation, and no survey-weighting or missing-data imputation strategy for student responses. The extracted country information reflects where first authors are affiliated or where example studies are situated, not a pooled student-level dataset.
